{{Citation needed|date=June 28, 2015, 13:33 (UTC)}} [[Image:Ashigalu.jpg|thumb|300px|{{ill|Ishikawa Ryusen|ja|石川流宣|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'s illustration of a servant in ''{{ill|Yamato Kōsaku Eshō|ja|大和耕作絵抄|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''.]] [[File:Jidai Matsuri 2009 143.jpg|300px|thumbnail|Servants during the {{ill|Jidai Matsuri|ja|時代祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}}]] '''Buke Hōkōnin''' (武家奉公人) refers to retainers of peasant or townsman origin who served the {{ill|buke|ja|武家|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (samurai class). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, such retainers were commonly referred to as simply {{ill|hōkōnin|ja|奉公人|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, including samurai retainers who served their masters. These individuals primarily handled subordinate tasks for warriors, such as carrying equipment, taking care of horses, and preparing meals. In some regions, they also participated in military service. By the Edo period, the term exclusively referred to non-samurai retainers like ''chūgen'' (中間) and ''komono'' (小者). == Types == ''Buke Hōkōnin'' included Ranks such as ''wakatō,'' ''chūgen,'' and ''komono.'' However, the terms varied significantly depending on the region, domain, and household, and distinctions were not rigid. For example, ''buke hōkōnin'' could sometimes include {{ill|ashigaru|ja|足軽|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, with their status either being below or above ''wakatō'' depending on the explanation.{{cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%8B%A5%E5%85%9A-153895|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240713000056/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%8B%A5%E5%85%9A-153895|title=Wakatō|publisher=Kotobank|archive-date=2024-07-13|access-date=2024-07-24}} Thus, the descriptions of ''wakatō,'' ''chūgen,'' and ''komono'' below are provided as general references. === Wakatō (若党) === The term ''wakatō'' appears in the text of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|qq=draft|qq=draft}}’s 1591 temporary {{ill|class system edict|ja|身分統制令|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, where "samurai" (侍) is thought to refer to ''wakatō.''{{Cite journal|author=Asuka Kato|url=https://doi.org/10.24707/aichikenshikenkyu.5.0_123|title=On Toyotomi Hideyoshi's Edict of 1591|journal=Aichi Historical Studies|volume=5|pages=123–130|doi=10.24707/aichikenshikenkyu.5.0_123}}{{cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%BA%AB%E5%88%86%E7%B5%B1%E5%88%B6%E4%BB%A4-872534|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231203072152/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%BA%AB%E5%88%86%E7%B5%B1%E5%88%B6%E4%BB%A4-872534|title=Class System Edict|publisher=Kotobank|archive-date=2023-12-03|access-date=2024-07-24}} The term originally referred to young retainers, as indicated in ''{{ill|Teijō Zatsuki|ja|貞丈雑記|qq=draft|qq=draft}},'' which states: "Wakatō refers to young samurai." Over time, however, it came to denote lower-Ranking samurai retainers.''World Encyclopedia,'' Hitachi Digital Heibonsha, 1998. In the {{ill|Azuchi-Momoyama period|ja|安土桃山時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the Edo period, ''wakatō'' served as low-Ranking attendants who carried out errands and guarded their masters. They were also called ''wakatō samurai'' and wore {{ill|daishō|ja|大小|qq=draft|qq=draft}} swords along with {{ill|haori|ja|羽織|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|hakama|ja|袴|qq=draft|qq=draft}} while accompanying their masters. Their annual stipend was about three ryō and one koku, leading them to be colloquially called "three-one samurai."''Definitive Edo Period Reference,'' Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha, 1994, ASIN B00N4T23DQ. === Chūgen (中間) === ''Chūgen'' were servants of non-samurai status, distinct from ''wakatō.'' They carried only one sword, often made of wood, and were prohibited from wearing silk garments. ''Chūgen'' were typically hired on a seasonal or one-time basis, often recruited through brokers in cities like {{ill|Edo|ja|江戸|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
Some ''chūgen'' held special roles, such as hereditary retainers in certain domains, who were permitted to bear surnames and swords.''Legacy Collection,'' Yonezawa City Library. === Komono (小者) === ''Komono'' were private servants who primarily performed miscellaneous tasks. They were also known as ''kobito'' or ''genan'' (下男). In the {{ill|Edo period}}, they assisted magistrates in arrest operations and were occasionally authorized to carry weapons like {{ill|jutte|ja|十手|qq=draft}}.
During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, ''komono'' served as battlefield assistants responsible for tasks like carrying supplies. == Changes in the Bakumatsu Period == In the {{ill|Bakumatsu|ja|幕末|qq=draft|qq=draft}} period, both the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and feudal domains abolished traditional musketeer units and established modern {{ill|infantry|ja|歩兵|qq=draft|qq=draft}} or firearm units. Some of these units were composed of ''chūgen'' recruits, reflecting a return to the recruitment practices of the Sengoku period. == Notable Figures == * {{ill|Ninomiya Sontoku|ja|二宮尊徳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Sasaki Kengaku|ja|佐々木顕発|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|ja|伊藤博文|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Yamagata Aritomo|ja|山縣有朋|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == See Also == * {{ill|Class System Edict|ja|身分統制令|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (issued by Toyotomi Hideyoshi) * {{ill|Shinōkōshō|ja|士農工商|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Hachiōji Sen'nin Dōshin|ja|八王子千人同心|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kabukimono|ja|かぶき者|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate Army|ja|幕府陸軍|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/une_gen/torimonochou.htm |title=Terminology in Crime Stories |date=20190101000000}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Samurai retainers}} {{translated page|ja|武家奉公人|version=103112657|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:11]] [[Category:Heian period]] [[Category:Kamakura period]] [[Category:Nanboku-chō period]] [[Category:Muromachi period]] [[Category:Sengoku period]] [[Category:Azuchi-Momoyama period]] [[Category:Edo period]] [[Category:Japanese warrior class]] [[Category:Domestic workers]] [[Category:Samurai retainers]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Samurai retainers]] [[en:Samurai retainers]] [[ja:武家奉公人]]

During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, ''komono'' served as battlefield assistants responsible for tasks like carrying supplies. == Changes in the Bakumatsu Period == In the {{ill|Bakumatsu|ja|幕末|qq=draft|qq=draft}} period, both the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and feudal domains abolished traditional musketeer units and established modern {{ill|infantry|ja|歩兵|qq=draft|qq=draft}} or firearm units. Some of these units were composed of ''chūgen'' recruits, reflecting a return to the recruitment practices of the Sengoku period. == Notable Figures == * {{ill|Ninomiya Sontoku|ja|二宮尊徳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Sasaki Kengaku|ja|佐々木顕発|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|ja|伊藤博文|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Yamagata Aritomo|ja|山縣有朋|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == See Also == * {{ill|Class System Edict|ja|身分統制令|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (issued by Toyotomi Hideyoshi) * {{ill|Shinōkōshō|ja|士農工商|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Hachiōji Sen'nin Dōshin|ja|八王子千人同心|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kabukimono|ja|かぶき者|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate Army|ja|幕府陸軍|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/une_gen/torimonochou.htm |title=Terminology in Crime Stories |date=20190101000000}} {{translated page|ja|武家奉公人|version=103112657|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:11]] [[Category:Heian period]] [[Category:Kamakura period]] [[Category:Nanboku-chō period]] [[Category:Muromachi period]] [[Category:Sengoku period]] [[Category:Azuchi-Momoyama period]] [[Category:Edo period]] [[Category:Japanese warrior class]] [[Category:Domestic workers]] [[Category:Samurai retainers]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Samurai retainers]] [[en:Samurai retainers]] [[ja:武家奉公人]]

{{Citation needed|date=June 28, 2015, 13:33 (UTC)}} [[Image:Ashigalu.jpg|thumb|300px|{{ill|Ishikawa Ryusen|ja|石川流宣|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'s illustration of a servant in ''{{ill|Yamato Kōsaku Eshō|ja|大和耕作絵抄|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''.]] [[File:Jidai Matsuri 2009 143.jpg|300px|thumbnail|Servants during the {{ill|Jidai Matsuri|ja|時代祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}}]] '''Buke Hōkōnin''' (武家奉公人) refers to retainers of peasant or townsman origin who served the {{ill|buke|ja|武家|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (samurai class). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, such retainers were commonly referred to as simply {{ill|hōkōnin|ja|奉公人|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, including samurai retainers who served their masters. These individuals primarily handled subordinate tasks for warriors, such as carrying equipment, taking care of horses, and preparing meals. In some regions, they also participated in military service. By the Edo period, the term exclusively referred to non-samurai retainers like ''chūgen'' (中間) and ''komono'' (小者). == Types == ''Buke Hōkōnin'' included Ranks such as ''wakatō,'' ''chūgen,'' and ''komono.'' However, the terms varied significantly depending on the region, domain, and household, and distinctions were not rigid. For example, ''buke hōkōnin'' could sometimes include {{ill|ashigaru|ja|足軽|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, with their status either being below or above ''wakatō'' depending on the explanation.{{cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%8B%A5%E5%85%9A-153895|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240713000056/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%8B%A5%E5%85%9A-153895|title=Wakatō|publisher=Kotobank|archive-date=2024-07-13|access-date=2024-07-24}} Thus, the descriptions of ''wakatō,'' ''chūgen,'' and ''komono'' below are provided as general references. === Wakatō (若党) === The term ''wakatō'' appears in the text of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|qq=draft|qq=draft}}’s 1591 temporary {{ill|class system edict|ja|身分統制令|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, where "samurai" (侍) is thought to refer to ''wakatō.''{{Cite journal|author=Asuka Kato|url=https://doi.org/10.24707/aichikenshikenkyu.5.0_123|title=On Toyotomi Hideyoshi's Edict of 1591|journal=Aichi Historical Studies|volume=5|pages=123–130|doi=10.24707/aichikenshikenkyu.5.0_123}}{{cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%BA%AB%E5%88%86%E7%B5%B1%E5%88%B6%E4%BB%A4-872534|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231203072152/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%BA%AB%E5%88%86%E7%B5%B1%E5%88%B6%E4%BB%A4-872534|title=Class System Edict|publisher=Kotobank|archive-date=2023-12-03|access-date=2024-07-24}} The term originally referred to young retainers, as indicated in ''{{ill|Teijō Zatsuki|ja|貞丈雑記|qq=draft|qq=draft}},'' which states: "Wakatō refers to young samurai." Over time, however, it came to denote lower-Ranking samurai retainers.''World Encyclopedia,'' Hitachi Digital Heibonsha, 1998. In the {{ill|Azuchi-Momoyama period|ja|安土桃山時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the Edo period, ''wakatō'' served as low-Ranking attendants who carried out errands and guarded their masters. They were also called ''wakatō samurai'' and wore {{ill|daishō|ja|大小|qq=draft|qq=draft}} swords along with {{ill|haori|ja|羽織|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|hakama|ja|袴|qq=draft|qq=draft}} while accompanying their masters. Their annual stipend was about three ryō and one koku, leading them to be colloquially called "three-one samurai."''Definitive Edo Period Reference,'' Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha, 1994, ASIN B00N4T23DQ. === Chūgen (中間) === ''Chūgen'' were servants of non-samurai status, distinct from ''wakatō.'' They carried only one sword, often made of wood, and were prohibited from wearing silk garments. ''Chūgen'' were typically hired on a seasonal or one-time basis, often recruited through brokers in cities like {{ill|Edo|ja|江戸|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
Some ''chūgen'' held special roles, such as hereditary retainers in certain domains, who were permitted to bear surnames and swords.''Legacy Collection,'' Yonezawa City Library. === Komono (小者) === ''Komono'' were private servants who primarily performed miscellaneous tasks. They were also known as ''kobito'' or ''genan'' (下男). In the {{ill|Edo period}}, they assisted magistrates in arrest operations and were occasionally authorized to carry weapons like {{ill|jutte|ja|十手|qq=draft}}.
During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, ''komono'' served as battlefield assistants responsible for tasks like carrying supplies. == Changes in the Bakumatsu Period == In the {{ill|Bakumatsu|ja|幕末|qq=draft|qq=draft}} period, both the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and feudal domains abolished traditional musketeer units and established modern {{ill|infantry|ja|歩兵|qq=draft|qq=draft}} or firearm units. Some of these units were composed of ''chūgen'' recruits, reflecting a return to the recruitment practices of the Sengoku period. == Notable Figures == * {{ill|Ninomiya Sontoku|ja|二宮尊徳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Sasaki Kengaku|ja|佐々木顕発|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|ja|伊藤博文|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Yamagata Aritomo|ja|山縣有朋|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == See Also == * {{ill|Class System Edict|ja|身分統制令|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (issued by Toyotomi Hideyoshi) * {{ill|Shinōkōshō|ja|士農工商|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Hachiōji Sen'nin Dōshin|ja|八王子千人同心|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kabukimono|ja|かぶき者|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate Army|ja|幕府陸軍|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/une_gen/torimonochou.htm |title=Terminology in Crime Stories |date=20190101000000}} {{translated page|ja|武家奉公人|version=103112657|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:11]] [[Category:Heian period]] [[Category:Kamakura period]] [[Category:Nanboku-chō period]] [[Category:Muromachi period]] [[Category:Sengoku period]] [[Category:Azuchi-Momoyama period]] [[Category:Edo period]] [[Category:Japanese warrior class]] [[Category:Domestic workers]] [[Category:Samurai retainers]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[sn:Samurai retainers]] [[en:Samurai retainers]] [[ja:武家奉公人]]

revision.text:
{{Citation needed|date=June 28, 2015, 13:33 (UTC)}} [[Image:Ashigalu.jpg|thumb|300px|{{ill|Ishikawa Ryusen|ja|石川流宣|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishikawa Ryusen|qq=}}'s illustration of a servant in ''{{ill|Yamato Kōsaku Eshō|ja|大和耕作絵抄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kōsaku Eshō|qq=}}''.]] [[File:Jidai Matsuri 2009 143.jpg|300px|thumbnail|Servants during the {{ill|Jidai Matsuri|ja|時代祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jidai Matsuri|qq=}}]] '''Buke Hōkōnin''' (武家奉公人) refers to retainers of peasant or townsman origin who served the {{ill|buke|ja|武家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/buke|qq=}} (samurai class). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, such retainers were commonly referred to as simply {{ill|hōkōnin|ja|奉公人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hōkōnin|qq=}}, including samurai retainers who served their masters. These individuals primarily handled subordinate tasks for warriors, such as carrying equipment, taking care of horses, and preparing meals. In some regions, they also participated in military service. By the Edo period, the term exclusively referred to non-samurai retainers like ''chūgen'' (中間) and ''komono'' (小者). == Types == ''Buke Hōkōnin'' included Ranks such as ''wakatō,'' ''chūgen,'' and ''komono.'' However, the terms varied significantly depending on the region, domain, and household, and distinctions were not rigid. For example, ''buke hōkōnin'' could sometimes include {{ill|ashigaru|ja|足軽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ashigaru|qq=}}, with their status either being below or above ''wakatō'' depending on the explanation.{{cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%8B%A5%E5%85%9A-153895|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240713000056/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%8B%A5%E5%85%9A-153895|title=Wakatō|publisher=Kotobank|archive-date=2024-07-13|access-date=2024-07-24}} Thus, the descriptions of ''wakatō,'' ''chūgen,'' and ''komono'' below are provided as general references. === Wakatō (若党) === The term ''wakatō'' appears in the text of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyotomi Hideyoshi|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyotomi Hideyoshi|qq=}}’s 1591 temporary {{ill|class system edict|ja|身分統制令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/class system edict|qq=}}, where "samurai" (侍) is thought to refer to ''wakatō.''{{Cite journal|author=Asuka Kato|url=https://doi.org/10.24707/aichikenshikenkyu.5.0_123|title=On Toyotomi Hideyoshi's Edict of 1591|journal=Aichi Historical Studies|volume=5|pages=123–130|doi=10.24707/aichikenshikenkyu.5.0_123}}{{cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%BA%AB%E5%88%86%E7%B5%B1%E5%88%B6%E4%BB%A4-872534|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231203072152/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%BA%AB%E5%88%86%E7%B5%B1%E5%88%B6%E4%BB%A4-872534|title=Class System Edict|publisher=Kotobank|archive-date=2023-12-03|access-date=2024-07-24}} The term originally referred to young retainers, as indicated in ''{{ill|Teijō Zatsuki|ja|貞丈雑記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Teijō Zatsuki|qq=}},'' which states: "Wakatō refers to young samurai." Over time, however, it came to denote lower-Ranking samurai retainers.''World Encyclopedia,'' Hitachi Digital Heibonsha, 1998. In the {{ill|Azuchi-Momoyama period|ja|安土桃山時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azuchi-Momoyama period|qq=}} and the Edo period, ''wakatō'' served as low-Ranking attendants who carried out errands and guarded their masters. They were also called ''wakatō samurai'' and wore {{ill|daishō|ja|大小|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/daishō|qq=}} swords along with {{ill|haori|ja|羽織|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/haori|qq=}} and {{ill|hakama|ja|袴|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hakama|qq=}} while accompanying their masters. Their annual stipend was about three ryō and one koku, leading them to be colloquially called "three-one samurai."''Definitive Edo Period Reference,'' Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha, 1994, ASIN B00N4T23DQ. === Chūgen (中間) === ''Chūgen'' were servants of non-samurai status, distinct from ''wakatō.'' They carried only one sword, often made of wood, and were prohibited from wearing silk garments. ''Chūgen'' were typically hired on a seasonal or one-time basis, often recruited through brokers in cities like {{ill|Edo|ja|江戸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo|qq=}}.
Some ''chūgen'' held special roles, such as hereditary retainers in certain domains, who were permitted to bear surnames and swords.''Legacy Collection,'' Yonezawa City Library. === Komono (小者) === ''Komono'' were private servants who primarily performed miscellaneous tasks. They were also known as ''kobito'' or ''genan'' (下男). In the {{ill|Edo period|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, they assisted magistrates in arrest operations and were occasionally authorized to carry weapons like {{ill|jutte|ja|十手|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/jutte|qq=}}.
During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, ''komono'' served as battlefield assistants responsible for tasks like carrying supplies. == Changes in the Bakumatsu Period == In the {{ill|Bakumatsu|ja|幕末|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bakumatsu|qq=}} period, both the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|qq=}} and feudal domains abolished traditional musketeer units and established modern {{ill|infantry|ja|歩兵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/infantry|qq=}} or firearm units. Some of these units were composed of ''chūgen'' recruits, reflecting a return to the recruitment practices of the Sengoku period. == Notable Figures == * {{ill|Ninomiya Sontoku|ja|二宮尊徳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninomiya Sontoku|qq=}} * {{ill|Sasaki Kengaku|ja|佐々木顕発|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasaki Kengaku|qq=}} * {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|ja|伊藤博文|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itō Hirobumi|qq=}} * {{ill|Yamagata Aritomo|ja|山縣有朋|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamagata Aritomo|qq=}} == See Also == * {{ill|Class System Edict|ja|身分統制令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Class System Edict|qq=}} (issued by Toyotomi Hideyoshi) * {{ill|Shinōkōshō|ja|士農工商|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinōkōshō|qq=}} * {{ill|Hachiōji Sen'nin Dōshin|ja|八王子千人同心|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Sen'nin Dōshin|qq=}} * {{ill|Kabukimono|ja|かぶき者|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kabukimono|qq=}} * {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate Army|ja|幕府陸軍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Shogunate Army|qq=}} == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/une_gen/torimonochou.htm |title=Terminology in Crime Stories |date=20190101000000}} {{translated page|ja|武家奉公人|version=103112657|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Heian period]] [[Category:Kamakura period]] [[Category:Nanboku-chō period]] [[Category:Muromachi period]] [[Category:Sengoku period]] [[Category:Azuchi-Momoyama period]] [[Category:Edo period]] [[Category:Japanese warrior class]] [[Category:Samurai retainers]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[sn:Samurai retainers]] [[en:Samurai retainers]] [[ja:武家奉公人]]

Some of these units were composed of ''chūgen'' recruits, reflecting a return to the recruitment practices of the Sengoku period. == Notable Figures == * {{ill|Ninomiya Sontoku|ja|二宮尊徳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninomiya Sontoku|qq=}} * {{ill|Sasaki Kengaku|ja|佐々木顕発|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasaki Kengaku|qq=}} * {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|ja|伊藤博文|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itō Hirobumi|qq=}} * {{ill|Yamagata Aritomo|ja|山縣有朋|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamagata Aritomo|qq=}} == See Also == * {{ill|Class System Edict|ja|身分統制令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Class System Edict|qq=}} (issued by Toyotomi Hideyoshi) * {{ill|Shinōkōshō|ja|士農工商|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinōkōshō|qq=}} * {{ill|Hachiōji Sen'nin Dōshin|ja|八王子千人同心|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Sen'nin Dōshin|qq=}} * {{ill|Kabukimono|ja|かぶき者|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kabukimono|qq=}} * {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate Army|ja|幕府陸軍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Shogunate Army|qq=}} == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/une_gen/torimonochou.htm |title=Terminology in Crime Stories |date=20190101000000}} {{translated page|ja|武家奉公人|version=103112657|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Heian period]] [[Category:Kamakura period]] [[Category:Nanboku-chō period]] [[Category:Muromachi period]] [[Category:Sengoku period]] [[Category:Azuchi-Momoyama period]] [[Category:Edo period]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[sn:Samurai retainers]] [[en:Samurai retainers]] [[ja:武家奉公人]]

revision.comment: Bot: replace {{ill}} with enwiki link via Wikidata
revision.text:
{{Citation needed|date=June 28, 2015, 13:33 (UTC)}} [[Image:Ashigalu.jpg|thumb|300px|{{ill|Ishikawa Ryusen|ja|石川流宣|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishikawa Ryusen|qq=}}'s illustration of a servant in ''{{ill|Yamato Kōsaku Eshō|ja|大和耕作絵抄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kōsaku Eshō|qq=}}''.]] [[File:Jidai Matsuri 2009 143.jpg|300px|thumbnail|Servants during the [[:en:Jidai Matsuri|Jidai Matsuri]]]] '''Buke Hōkōnin''' (武家奉公人) refers to retainers of peasant or townsman origin who served the {{ill|buke|ja|武家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/buke|qq=}} (samurai class). During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], such retainers were commonly referred to as simply {{ill|hōkōnin|ja|奉公人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hōkōnin|qq=}}, including samurai retainers who served their masters. These individuals primarily handled subordinate tasks for warriors, such as carrying equipment, taking care of horses, and preparing meals. In some regions, they also participated in military service. By the Edo period, the term exclusively referred to non-samurai retainers like ''chūgen'' (中間) and ''komono'' (小者). == Types == ''Buke Hōkōnin'' included Ranks such as ''wakatō,'' ''chūgen,'' and ''komono.'' However, the terms varied significantly depending on the region, domain, and household, and distinctions were not rigid. For example, ''buke hōkōnin'' could sometimes include [[:en:Ashigaru|ashigaru]], with their status either being below or above ''wakatō'' depending on the explanation.{{cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%8B%A5%E5%85%9A-153895|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240713000056/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%8B%A5%E5%85%9A-153895|title=Wakatō|publisher=Kotobank|archive-date=2024-07-13|access-date=2024-07-24}} Thus, the descriptions of ''wakatō,'' ''chūgen,'' and ''komono'' below are provided as general references. === Wakatō (若党) === The term ''wakatō'' appears in the text of [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]’s 1591 temporary [[:en:Separation Edict|class system edict]], where "samurai" (侍) is thought to refer to ''wakatō.''{{Cite journal|author=Asuka Kato|url=https://doi.org/10.24707/aichikenshikenkyu.5.0_123|title=On Toyotomi Hideyoshi's Edict of 1591|journal=Aichi Historical Studies|volume=5|pages=123–130|doi=10.24707/aichikenshikenkyu.5.0_123}}{{cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%BA%AB%E5%88%86%E7%B5%B1%E5%88%B6%E4%BB%A4-872534|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231203072152/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%BA%AB%E5%88%86%E7%B5%B1%E5%88%B6%E4%BB%A4-872534|title=Class System Edict|publisher=Kotobank|archive-date=2023-12-03|access-date=2024-07-24}} The term originally referred to young retainers, as indicated in ''{{ill|Teijō Zatsuki|ja|貞丈雑記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Teijō Zatsuki|qq=}},'' which states: "Wakatō refers to young samurai." Over time, however, it came to denote lower-Ranking samurai retainers.''World Encyclopedia,'' Hitachi Digital Heibonsha, 1998. In the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi-Momoyama period]] and the Edo period, ''wakatō'' served as low-Ranking attendants who carried out errands and guarded their masters. They were also called ''wakatō samurai'' and wore {{ill|daishō|ja|大小|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/daishō|qq=}} swords along with [[:en:Haori|haori]] and [[:en:Hakama|hakama]] while accompanying their masters. Their annual stipend was about three ryō and one koku, leading them to be colloquially called "three-one samurai."''Definitive Edo Period Reference,'' Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha, 1994, ASIN B00N4T23DQ. === Chūgen (中間) === ''Chūgen'' were servants of non-samurai status, distinct from ''wakatō.'' They carried only one sword, often made of wood, and were prohibited from wearing silk garments. ''Chūgen'' were typically hired on a seasonal or one-time basis, often recruited through brokers in cities like [[:en:Edo|Edo]].
Some ''chūgen'' held special roles, such as hereditary retainers in certain domains, who were permitted to bear surnames and swords.''Legacy Collection,'' Yonezawa City Library. === Komono (小者) === ''Komono'' were private servants who primarily performed miscellaneous tasks. They were also known as ''kobito'' or ''genan'' (下男). In the {{ill|Edo period|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, they assisted magistrates in arrest operations and were occasionally authorized to carry weapons like [[:en:Jitte|jutte]].
During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, ''komono'' served as battlefield assistants responsible for tasks like carrying supplies. == Changes in the Bakumatsu Period == In the [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] period, both the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|qq=}} and feudal domains abolished traditional musketeer units and established modern [[:en:Infantry|infantry]] or firearm units. Some of these units were composed of ''chūgen'' recruits, reflecting a return to the recruitment practices of the Sengoku period. == Notable Figures == * [[:en:Ninomiya Sontoku|Ninomiya Sontoku]] * {{ill|Sasaki Kengaku|ja|佐々木顕発|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasaki Kengaku|qq=}} * [[:en:Itō Hirobumi|Itō Hirobumi]] * [[:en:Yamagata Aritomo|Yamagata Aritomo]] == See Also == * [[:en:Separation Edict|Class System Edict]] (issued by Toyotomi Hideyoshi) * [[:en:Four occupations|Shinōkōshō]] * {{ill|Hachiōji Sen'nin Dōshin|ja|八王子千人同心|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Sen'nin Dōshin|qq=}} * [[:en:Kabukimono|Kabukimono]] * {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate Army|ja|幕府陸軍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Shogunate Army|qq=}} == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/une_gen/torimonochou.htm |title=Terminology in Crime Stories |date=20190101000000}} {{translated page|ja|武家奉公人|version=103112657|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Heian period]] [[Category:Kamakura period]] [[Category:Nanboku-chō period]] [[Category:Muromachi period]] [[Category:Sengoku period]] [[Category:Azuchi-Momoyama period]] [[Category:Edo period]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[sn:Samurai retainers]] [[en:Samurai retainers]] [[ja:武家奉公人]]

Over time, however, it came to denote lower-Ranking samurai retainers.''World Encyclopedia,'' Hitachi Digital Heibonsha, 1998. revision.text:
{{Citation needed|date=June 28, 2015, 13:33 (UTC)}} [[Image:Ashigalu.jpg|thumb|300px|{{ill|Ishikawa Ryusen|ja|石川流宣|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishikawa Ryusen|qq=}}'s illustration of a servant in ''{{ill|Yamato Kōsaku Eshō|ja|大和耕作絵抄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kōsaku Eshō|qq=}}''.]] [[File:Jidai Matsuri 2009 143.jpg|300px|thumbnail|Servants during the [[:en:Jidai Matsuri|Jidai Matsuri]]]] '''Buke Hōkōnin''' (武家奉公人) refers to retainers of peasant or townsman origin who served the {{ill|buke|ja|武家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/buke|qq=}} (samurai class). During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], such retainers were commonly referred to as simply {{ill|hōkōnin|ja|奉公人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hōkōnin|qq=}}, including samurai retainers who served their masters. These individuals primarily handled subordinate tasks for warriors, such as carrying equipment, taking care of horses, and preparing meals. In some regions, they also participated in military service. By the Edo period, the term exclusively referred to non-samurai retainers like ''chūgen'' (中間) and ''komono'' (小者). == Types == ''Buke Hōkōnin'' included Ranks such as ''wakatō,'' ''chūgen,'' and ''komono.'' However, the terms varied significantly depending on the region, domain, and household, and distinctions were not rigid. For example, ''buke hōkōnin'' could sometimes include [[:en:Ashigaru|ashigaru]], with their status either being below or above ''wakatō'' depending on the explanation.{{cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%8B%A5%E5%85%9A-153895|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240713000056/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%8B%A5%E5%85%9A-153895|title=Wakatō|publisher=Kotobank|archive-date=2024-07-13|access-date=2024-07-24}} Thus, the descriptions of ''wakatō,'' ''chūgen,'' and ''komono'' below are provided as general references. === Wakatō (若党) === The term ''wakatō'' appears in the text of [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]’s 1591 temporary [[:en:Separation Edict|class system edict]], where "samurai" (侍) is thought to refer to ''wakatō.''{{Cite journal|author=Asuka Kato|url=https://doi.org/10.24707/aichikenshikenkyu.5.0_123|title=On Toyotomi Hideyoshi's Edict of 1591|journal=Aichi Historical Studies|volume=5|pages=123–130|doi=10.24707/aichikenshikenkyu.5.0_123}}{{cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%BA%AB%E5%88%86%E7%B5%B1%E5%88%B6%E4%BB%A4-872534|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231203072152/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%BA%AB%E5%88%86%E7%B5%B1%E5%88%B6%E4%BB%A4-872534|title=Class System Edict|publisher=Kotobank|archive-date=2023-12-03|access-date=2024-07-24}} The term originally referred to young retainers, as indicated in ''{{ill|Teijō Zatsuki|ja|貞丈雑記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Teijō Zatsuki|qq=}},'' which states: "Wakatō refers to young samurai." Over time, however, it came to denote lower-Ranking samurai retainers.''World Encyclopedia,'' Hitachi Digital Heibonsha, 1998. In the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi-Momoyama period]] and the Edo period, ''wakatō'' served as low-Ranking attendants who carried out errands and guarded their masters. They were also called ''wakatō samurai'' and wore {{ill|daishō|ja|大小|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/daishō|qq=}} swords along with [[:en:Haori|haori]] and [[:en:Hakama|hakama]] while accompanying their masters. Their annual stipend was about three ryō and one koku, leading them to be colloquially called "three-one samurai."''Definitive Edo Period Reference,'' Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha, 1994, ASIN B00N4T23DQ. === Chūgen (中間) === ''Chūgen'' were servants of non-samurai status, distinct from ''wakatō.'' They carried only one sword, often made of wood, and were prohibited from wearing silk garments. ''Chūgen'' were typically hired on a seasonal or one-time basis, often recruited through brokers in cities like [[:en:Edo|Edo]].
Some ''chūgen'' held special roles, such as hereditary retainers in certain domains, who were permitted to bear surnames and swords.''Legacy Collection,'' Yonezawa City Library. === Komono (小者) === ''Komono'' were private servants who primarily performed miscellaneous tasks. They were also known as ''kobito'' or ''genan'' (下男). In the {{ill|Edo period|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, they assisted magistrates in arrest operations and were occasionally authorized to carry weapons like [[:en:Jitte|jutte]].
During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, ''komono'' served as battlefield assistants responsible for tasks like carrying supplies. == Changes in the Bakumatsu Period == In the [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] period, both the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|qq=}} and feudal domains abolished traditional musketeer units and established modern [[:en:Infantry|infantry]] or firearm units. Some of these units were composed of ''chūgen'' recruits, reflecting a return to the recruitment practices of the Sengoku period. == Notable Figures == * [[:en:Ninomiya Sontoku|Ninomiya Sontoku]] * {{ill|Sasaki Kengaku|ja|佐々木顕発|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasaki Kengaku|qq=}} * [[:en:Itō Hirobumi|Itō Hirobumi]] * [[:en:Yamagata Aritomo|Yamagata Aritomo]] == See Also == * [[:en:Separation Edict|Class System Edict]] (issued by Toyotomi Hideyoshi) * [[:en:Four occupations|Shinōkōshō]] * {{ill|Hachiōji Sen'nin Dōshin|ja|八王子千人同心|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Sen'nin Dōshin|qq=}} * [[:en:Kabukimono|Kabukimono]] * {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate Army|ja|幕府陸軍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Shogunate Army|qq=}} == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/une_gen/torimonochou.htm |title=Terminology in Crime Stories |date=20190101000000}} {{translated page|ja|武家奉公人|version=103112657|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Heian period]] [[Category:Kamakura period]] [[Category:Nanboku-chō period]] [[Category:Muromachi period]] [[Category:Sengoku period]] [[Category:Azuchi-Momoyama period]] [[Category:Edo period]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[sn:Samurai retainers]] [[en:Samurai retainers]] [[ja:武家奉公人]] [[Category:Azuchi–Momoyama period]] [[Category:Domestic workers]] [[Category:日本の武家]]

Over time, however, it came to denote lower-Ranking samurai retainers.''World Encyclopedia,'' Hitachi Digital Heibonsha, 1998. In the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi-Momoyama period]] and the Edo period, ''wakatō'' served as low-Ranking attendants who carried out errands and guarded their masters. They were also called ''wakatō samurai'' and wore {{ill|daishō|ja|大小|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/daishō|qq=}} swords along with [[:en:Haori|haori]] and [[:en:Hakama|hakama]] while accompanying their masters. Their annual stipend was about three ryō and one koku, leading them to be colloquially called "three-one samurai."''Definitive Edo Period Reference,'' Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha, 1994, ASIN B00N4T23DQ. === Chūgen (中間) === ''Chūgen'' were servants of non-samurai status, distinct from ''wakatō.'' They carried only one sword, often made of wood, and were prohibited from wearing silk garments. ''Chūgen'' were typically hired on a seasonal or one-time basis, often recruited through brokers in cities like [[:en:Edo|Edo]].
Some ''chūgen'' held special roles, such as hereditary retainers in certain domains, who were permitted to bear surnames and swords.''Legacy Collection,'' Yonezawa City Library. === Komono (小者) === ''Komono'' were private servants who primarily performed miscellaneous tasks. They were also known as ''kobito'' or ''genan'' (下男). In the {{ill|Edo period|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, they assisted magistrates in arrest operations and were occasionally authorized to carry weapons like [[:en:Jitte|jutte]].
During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, ''komono'' served as battlefield assistants responsible for tasks like carrying supplies. == Changes in the Bakumatsu Period == In the [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] period, both the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and feudal domains abolished traditional musketeer units and established modern [[:en:Infantry|infantry]] or firearm units. Some of these units were composed of ''chūgen'' recruits, reflecting a return to the recruitment practices of the Sengoku period. == Notable Figures == * [[:en:Ninomiya Sontoku|Ninomiya Sontoku]] * {{ill|Sasaki Kengaku|ja|佐々木顕発|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasaki Kengaku|qq=}} * [[:en:Itō Hirobumi|Itō Hirobumi]] * [[:en:Yamagata Aritomo|Yamagata Aritomo]] == See Also == * [[:en:Separation Edict|Class System Edict]] (issued by Toyotomi Hideyoshi) * [[:en:Four occupations|Shinōkōshō]] * {{ill|Hachiōji Sen'nin Dōshin|ja|八王子千人同心|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Sen'nin Dōshin|qq=}} * [[:en:Kabukimono|Kabukimono]] * {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate Army|ja|幕府陸軍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Shogunate Army|qq=}} == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/une_gen/torimonochou.htm |title=Terminology in Crime Stories |date=20190101000000}} {{translated page|ja|武家奉公人|version=103112657|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Heian period]] [[Category:Kamakura period]] [[Category:Nanboku-chō period]] [[Category:Muromachi period]] [[Category:Sengoku period]] [[Category:Azuchi-Momoyama period]] [[Category:Edo period]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[sn:Samurai retainers]] [[ja:武家奉公人]] [[Category:Azuchi–Momoyama period]] [[Category:Domestic workers]] [[Category:日本の武家]]

revision.text:
{{Citation needed|date=June 28, 2015, 13:33 (UTC)}} [[Image:Ashigalu.jpg|thumb|300px|{{ill|Ishikawa Ryusen|ja|石川流宣|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishikawa Ryusen|qq=}}'s illustration of a servant in ''{{ill|Yamato Kōsaku Eshō|ja|大和耕作絵抄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kōsaku Eshō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''.]] [[File:Jidai Matsuri 2009 143.jpg|300px|thumbnail|Servants during the [[:en:Jidai Matsuri|Jidai Matsuri]]]] '''Buke Hōkōnin''' (武家奉公人) refers to retainers of peasant or townsman origin who served the {{ill|buke|ja|武家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/buke|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (samurai class). During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], such retainers were commonly referred to as simply {{ill|hōkōnin|ja|奉公人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hōkōnin|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 奉公}}, including samurai retainers who served their masters. These individuals primarily handled subordinate tasks for warriors, such as carrying equipment, taking care of horses, and preparing meals. In some regions, they also participated in military service. By the Edo period, the term exclusively referred to non-samurai retainers like ''chūgen'' (中間) and ''komono'' (小者). == Types == ''Buke Hōkōnin'' included Ranks such as ''wakatō,'' ''chūgen,'' and ''komono.'' However, the terms varied significantly depending on the region, domain, and household, and distinctions were not rigid. For example, ''buke hōkōnin'' could sometimes include [[:en:Ashigaru|ashigaru]], with their status either being below or above ''wakatō'' depending on the explanation.{{cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%8B%A5%E5%85%9A-153895|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240713000056/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%8B%A5%E5%85%9A-153895|title=Wakatō|publisher=Kotobank|archive-date=2024-07-13|access-date=2024-07-24}} Thus, the descriptions of ''wakatō,'' ''chūgen,'' and ''komono'' below are provided as general references. === Wakatō (若党) === The term ''wakatō'' appears in the text of [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]’s 1591 temporary [[:en:Separation Edict|class system edict]], where "samurai" (侍) is thought to refer to ''wakatō.''{{Cite journal|author=Asuka Kato|url=https://doi.org/10.24707/aichikenshikenkyu.5.0_123|title=On Toyotomi Hideyoshi's Edict of 1591|journal=Aichi Historical Studies|volume=5|pages=123–130|doi=10.24707/aichikenshikenkyu.5.0_123}}{{cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%BA%AB%E5%88%86%E7%B5%B1%E5%88%B6%E4%BB%A4-872534|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231203072152/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%BA%AB%E5%88%86%E7%B5%B1%E5%88%B6%E4%BB%A4-872534|title=Class System Edict|publisher=Kotobank|archive-date=2023-12-03|access-date=2024-07-24}} The term originally referred to young retainers, as indicated in ''{{ill|Teijō Zatsuki|ja|貞丈雑記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Teijō Zatsuki|qq=}},'' which states: "Wakatō refers to young samurai." Over time, however, it came to denote lower-Ranking samurai retainers.''World Encyclopedia,'' Hitachi Digital Heibonsha, 1998. In the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi-Momoyama period]] and the Edo period, ''wakatō'' served as low-Ranking attendants who carried out errands and guarded their masters. They were also called ''wakatō samurai'' and wore {{ill|daishō|ja|大小|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/daishō|qq=}} swords along with [[:en:Haori|haori]] and [[:en:Hakama|hakama]] while accompanying their masters. Their annual stipend was about three ryō and one koku, leading them to be colloquially called "three-one samurai."''Definitive Edo Period Reference,'' Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha, 1994, ASIN B00N4T23DQ. === Chūgen (中間) === ''Chūgen'' were servants of non-samurai status, distinct from ''wakatō.'' They carried only one sword, often made of wood, and were prohibited from wearing silk garments. ''Chūgen'' were typically hired on a seasonal or one-time basis, often recruited through brokers in cities like [[:en:Edo|Edo]].
Some ''chūgen'' held special roles, such as hereditary retainers in certain domains, who were permitted to bear surnames and swords.''Legacy Collection,'' Yonezawa City Library. === Komono (小者) === ''Komono'' were private servants who primarily performed miscellaneous tasks. They were also known as ''kobito'' or ''genan'' (下男). In the {{ill|Edo period|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, they assisted magistrates in arrest operations and were occasionally authorized to carry weapons like [[:en:Jitte|jutte]].
During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, ''komono'' served as battlefield assistants responsible for tasks like carrying supplies. == Changes in the Bakumatsu Period == In the [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] period, both the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and feudal domains abolished traditional musketeer units and established modern [[:en:Infantry|infantry]] or firearm units. Some of these units were composed of ''chūgen'' recruits, reflecting a return to the recruitment practices of the Sengoku period. == Notable Figures == * [[:en:Ninomiya Sontoku|Ninomiya Sontoku]] * {{ill|Sasaki Kengaku|ja|佐々木顕発|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasaki Kengaku|qq=}} * [[:en:Itō Hirobumi|Itō Hirobumi]] * [[:en:Yamagata Aritomo|Yamagata Aritomo]] == See Also == * [[:en:Separation Edict|Class System Edict]] (issued by Toyotomi Hideyoshi) * [[:en:Four occupations|Shinōkōshō]] * {{ill|Hachiōji Sen'nin Dōshin|ja|八王子千人同心|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Sen'nin Dōshin|qq=}} * [[:en:Kabukimono|Kabukimono]] * {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate Army|ja|幕府陸軍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Shogunate Army|qq=}} == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/une_gen/torimonochou.htm |title=Terminology in Crime Stories |date=20190101000000}} {{translated page|ja|武家奉公人|version=103112657|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Heian period]] [[Category:Kamakura period]] [[Category:Nanboku-chō period]] [[Category:Muromachi period]] [[Category:Sengoku period]] [[Category:Azuchi-Momoyama period]] [[Category:Edo period]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[sn:Samurai retainers]] [[ja:武家奉公人]] [[Category:Azuchi–Momoyama period]] [[Category:Domestic workers]] [[Category:Warrior Clans of Japan]]

Over time, however, it came to denote lower-Ranking samurai retainers.''World Encyclopedia,'' Hitachi Digital Heibonsha, 1998. They were also called ''wakatō samurai'' and wore {{ill|daishō|ja|大小|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/daishō|qq=}} swords along with [[:en:Haori|haori]] and [[:en:Hakama|hakama]] while accompanying their masters. Their annual stipend was about three ryō and one koku, leading them to be colloquially called "three-one samurai."''Definitive Edo Period Reference,'' Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha, 1994, ASIN B00N4T23DQ. === Chūgen (中間) === ''Chūgen'' were servants of non-samurai status, distinct from ''wakatō.'' In the {{ill|Edo period|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, they assisted magistrates in arrest operations and were occasionally authorized to carry weapons like [[:en:Jitte|jutte]].
During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, ''komono'' served as battlefield assistants responsible for tasks like carrying supplies. == Changes in the Bakumatsu Period == In the [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] period, both the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and feudal domains abolished traditional musketeer units and established modern [[:en:Infantry|infantry]] or firearm units. Some of these units were composed of ''chūgen'' recruits, reflecting a return to the recruitment practices of the Sengoku period. == Notable Figures == * [[:en:Ninomiya Sontoku|Ninomiya Sontoku]] * {{ill|Sasaki Kengaku|ja|佐々木顕発|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasaki Kengaku|qq=|1=Sasaki Akinobu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6526523}} * [[:en:Itō Hirobumi|Itō Hirobumi]] * [[:en:Yamagata Aritomo|Yamagata Aritomo]] == See Also == * [[:en:Separation Edict|Class System Edict]] (issued by Toyotomi Hideyoshi) * [[:en:Four occupations|Shinōkōshō]] * {{ill|Hachiōji Sen'nin Dōshin|ja|八王子千人同心|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Sen'nin Dōshin|qq=|1=Hachiōji Sen'nin Dōshin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11391560}} * [[:en:Kabukimono|Kabukimono]] * {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate Army|ja|幕府陸軍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Shogunate Army|qq=|1=Tokugawa shogunate army|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11481798}} == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/une_gen/torimonochou.htm |title=Terminology in Crime Stories |date=20190101000000}} {{translated page|ja|武家奉公人|version=103112657|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Heian period]] [[Category:Kamakura period]] [[Category:Nanboku-chō period]] [[Category:Muromachi period]] [[Category:Sengoku period]] [[Category:Azuchi-Momoyama period]] [[Category:Edo period]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[sn:Samurai retainers]] [[ja:武家奉公人]] [[Category:Azuchi–Momoyama period]] [[Category:Domestic workers]] [[Category:Warrior Clans of Japan]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11545898]] Wikidata name: buke hōkōnin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Citation needed|date=June 28, 2015, 13:33 (UTC)}} [[Image:Ashigalu.jpg|thumb|300px|{{ill|Ishikawa Ryusen|ja|石川流宣|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishikawa Ryusen|qq=|1=Ishikawa Tomonobu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11585888}}'s illustration of a servant in ''{{ill|Yamato Kōsaku Eshō|ja|大和耕作絵抄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kōsaku Eshō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''.]] [[File:Jidai Matsuri 2009 143.jpg|300px|thumbnail|Servants during the [[:en:Jidai Matsuri|Jidai Matsuri]]]] '''Buke Hōkōnin''' (武家奉公人) refers to retainers of peasant or townsman origin who served the {{ill|buke|ja|武家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/buke|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (samurai class). During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], such retainers were commonly referred to as simply {{ill|hōkōnin|ja|奉公人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hōkōnin|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 奉公|1=hōkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11445490}}, including samurai retainers who served their masters. These individuals primarily handled subordinate tasks for warriors, such as carrying equipment, taking care of horses, and preparing meals. In some regions, they also participated in military service. By the Edo period, the term exclusively referred to non-samurai retainers like ''chūgen'' (中間) and ''komono'' (小者). == Types == ''Buke Hōkōnin'' included Ranks such as ''wakatō,'' ''chūgen,'' and ''komono.'' However, the terms varied significantly depending on the region, domain, and household, and distinctions were not rigid. For example, ''buke hōkōnin'' could sometimes include [[:en:Ashigaru|ashigaru]], with their status either being below or above ''wakatō'' depending on the explanation.{{cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%8B%A5%E5%85%9A-153895|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240713000056/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%8B%A5%E5%85%9A-153895|title=Wakatō|publisher=Kotobank|archive-date=2024-07-13|access-date=2024-07-24}} Thus, the descriptions of ''wakatō,'' ''chūgen,'' and ''komono'' below are provided as general references. === Wakatō (若党) === The term ''wakatō'' appears in the text of [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]’s 1591 temporary [[:en:Separation Edict|class system edict]], where "samurai" (侍) is thought to refer to ''wakatō.''{{Cite journal|author=Asuka Kato|url=https://doi.org/10.24707/aichikenshikenkyu.5.0_123|title=On Toyotomi Hideyoshi's Edict of 1591|journal=Aichi Historical Studies|volume=5|pages=123–130|doi=10.24707/aichikenshikenkyu.5.0_123}}{{cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%BA%AB%E5%88%86%E7%B5%B1%E5%88%B6%E4%BB%A4-872534|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231203072152/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%BA%AB%E5%88%86%E7%B5%B1%E5%88%B6%E4%BB%A4-872534|title=Class System Edict|publisher=Kotobank|archive-date=2023-12-03|access-date=2024-07-24}} The term originally referred to young retainers, as indicated in ''{{ill|Teijō Zatsuki|ja|貞丈雑記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Teijō Zatsuki|qq=|1=Teijō Zatsuki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30933533}},'' which states: "Wakatō refers to young samurai." Over time, however, it came to denote lower-Ranking samurai retainers.''World Encyclopedia,'' Hitachi Digital Heibonsha, 1998. In the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi-Momoyama period]] and the Edo period, ''wakatō'' served as low-Ranking attendants who carried out errands and guarded their masters. They were also called ''wakatō samurai'' and wore {{ill|daishō|ja|大小|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/daishō|qq=|1=Large and Small|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10935019}} swords along with [[:en:Haori|haori]] and [[:en:Hakama|hakama]] while accompanying their masters. Their annual stipend was about three ryō and one koku, leading them to be colloquially called "three-one samurai."''Definitive Edo Period Reference,'' Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha, 1994, ASIN B00N4T23DQ. === Chūgen (中間) === ''Chūgen'' were servants of non-samurai status, distinct from ''wakatō.'' They carried only one sword, often made of wood, and were prohibited from wearing silk garments. ''Chūgen'' were typically hired on a seasonal or one-time basis, often recruited through brokers in cities like [[:en:Edo|Edo]].
Some ''chūgen'' held special roles, such as hereditary retainers in certain domains, who were permitted to bear surnames and swords.''Legacy Collection,'' Yonezawa City Library. === Komono (小者) === ''Komono'' were private servants who primarily performed miscellaneous tasks. They were also known as ''kobito'' or ''genan'' (下男). In the {{ill|Edo period|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, they assisted magistrates in arrest operations and were occasionally authorized to carry weapons like [[:en:Jitte|jutte]].
During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, ''komono'' served as battlefield assistants responsible for tasks like carrying supplies. == Changes in the Bakumatsu Period == In the [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] period, both the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and feudal domains abolished traditional musketeer units and established modern [[:en:Infantry|infantry]] or firearm units. Some of these units were composed of ''chūgen'' recruits, reflecting a return to the recruitment practices of the Sengoku period. == Notable Figures == * [[:en:Ninomiya Sontoku|Ninomiya Sontoku]] * {{ill|Sasaki Kengaku|ja|佐々木顕発|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasaki Kengaku|qq=|1=Sasaki Akinobu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6526523}} * [[:en:Itō Hirobumi|Itō Hirobumi]] * [[:en:Yamagata Aritomo|Yamagata Aritomo]] == See Also == * [[:en:Separation Edict|Class System Edict]] (issued by Toyotomi Hideyoshi) * [[:en:Four occupations|Shinōkōshō]] * {{ill|Hachiōji Sen'nin Dōshin|ja|八王子千人同心|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Sen'nin Dōshin|qq=|1=Hachiōji Sennin Dōshin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11391560}} * [[:en:Kabukimono|Kabukimono]] * {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate Army|ja|幕府陸軍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Shogunate Army|qq=|1=Tokugawa shogunate army|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11481798}} == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/une_gen/torimonochou.htm |title=Terminology in Crime Stories |date=20190101000000}} {{translated page|ja|武家奉公人|version=103112657|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Heian period]] [[Category:Kamakura period]] [[Category:Nanboku-chō period]] [[Category:Muromachi period]] [[Category:Sengoku period]] [[Category:Azuchi-Momoyama period]] [[Category:Edo period]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[sn:Samurai retainers]] [[ja:武家奉公人]] [[Category:Azuchi–Momoyama period]] [[Category:Domestic workers]] [[Category:Warrior Clans of Japan]]

During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], such retainers were commonly referred to as simply {{ill|hōkōnin|ja|奉公人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hōkōnin|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 奉公|1=hōkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11445490}}, including samurai retainers who served their masters. These individuals primarily handled subordinate tasks for warriors, such as carrying equipment, taking care of horses, and preparing meals. In some regions, they also participated in military service. By the Edo period, the term exclusively referred to non-samurai retainers like ''chūgen'' (中間) and ''komono'' (小者). == Types == ''Buke Hōkōnin'' included Ranks such as ''wakatō,'' ''chūgen,'' and ''komono.'' However, the terms varied significantly depending on the region, domain, and household, and distinctions were not rigid. For example, ''buke hōkōnin'' could sometimes include [[:en:Ashigaru|ashigaru]], with their status either being below or above ''wakatō'' depending on the explanation.{{cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%8B%A5%E5%85%9A-153895|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240713000056/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%8B%A5%E5%85%9A-153895|title=Wakatō|publisher=Kotobank|archive-date=2024-07-13|access-date=2024-07-24}} Thus, the descriptions of ''wakatō,'' ''chūgen,'' and ''komono'' below are provided as general references. === Wakatō (若党) === The term ''wakatō'' appears in the text of [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]’s 1591 temporary [[:en:Separation Edict|class system edict]], where "samurai" (侍) is thought to refer to ''wakatō.''{{Cite journal|author=Asuka Kato|url=https://doi.org/10.24707/aichikenshikenkyu.5.0_123|title=On Toyotomi Hideyoshi's Edict of 1591|journal=Aichi Historical Studies|volume=5|pages=123–130|doi=10.24707/aichikenshikenkyu.5.0_123}}{{cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%BA%AB%E5%88%86%E7%B5%B1%E5%88%B6%E4%BB%A4-872534|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231203072152/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%BA%AB%E5%88%86%E7%B5%B1%E5%88%B6%E4%BB%A4-872534|title=Class System Edict|publisher=Kotobank|archive-date=2023-12-03|access-date=2024-07-24}} The term originally referred to young retainers, as indicated in ''{{ill|Teijō Zatsuki|ja|貞丈雑記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Teijō Zatsuki|qq=|1=Teijō Zatsuki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30933533}},'' which states: "Wakatō refers to young samurai." Over time, however, it came to denote lower-Ranking samurai retainers.''World Encyclopedia,'' Hitachi Digital Heibonsha, 1998. In the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi-Momoyama period]] and the Edo period, ''wakatō'' served as low-Ranking attendants who carried out errands and guarded their masters. They were also called ''wakatō samurai'' and wore {{ill|daishō|ja|大小|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/daishō|qq=|1=Large and Small|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10935019}} swords along with [[:en:Haori|haori]] and [[:en:Hakama|hakama]] while accompanying their masters. Their annual stipend was about three ryō and one koku, leading them to be colloquially called "three-one samurai."''Definitive Edo Period Reference,'' Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha, 1994, ASIN B00N4T23DQ. === Chūgen (中間) === ''Chūgen'' were servants of non-samurai status, distinct from ''wakatō.'' They carried only one sword, often made of wood, and were prohibited from wearing silk garments. ''Chūgen'' were typically hired on a seasonal or one-time basis, often recruited through brokers in cities like [[:en:Edo|Edo]].
Some ''chūgen'' held special roles, such as hereditary retainers in certain domains, who were permitted to bear surnames and swords.''Legacy Collection,'' Yonezawa City Library. === Komono (小者) === ''Komono'' were private servants who primarily performed miscellaneous tasks. They were also known as ''kobito'' or ''genan'' (下男). In the {{ill|Edo period|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, they assisted magistrates in arrest operations and were occasionally authorized to carry weapons like [[:en:Jitte|jutte]].
During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, ''komono'' served as battlefield assistants responsible for tasks like carrying supplies. == Changes in the Bakumatsu Period == In the [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] period, both the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and feudal domains abolished traditional musketeer units and established modern [[:en:Infantry|infantry]] or firearm units. Some of these units were composed of ''chūgen'' recruits, reflecting a return to the recruitment practices of the Sengoku period. == Notable Figures == * [[:en:Ninomiya Sontoku|Ninomiya Sontoku]] * {{ill|Sasaki Kengaku|ja|佐々木顕発|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasaki Kengaku|qq=|1=Sasaki Akinobu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6526523}} * [[:en:Itō Hirobumi|Itō Hirobumi]] * [[:en:Yamagata Aritomo|Yamagata Aritomo]] == See Also == * [[:en:Separation Edict|Class System Edict]] (issued by Toyotomi Hideyoshi) * [[:en:Four occupations|Shinōkōshō]] * {{ill|Hachiōji Sen'nin Dōshin|ja|八王子千人同心|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Sen'nin Dōshin|qq=|1=Hachiōji Sennin Dōshin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11391560}} * [[:en:Kabukimono|Kabukimono]] * {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate Army|ja|幕府陸軍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Shogunate Army|qq=|1=Tokugawa shogunate army|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11481798}} == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/une_gen/torimonochou.htm |title=Terminology in Crime Stories |date=20190101000000}} {{translated page|ja|武家奉公人|version=103112657|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Heian period]] [[Category:Kamakura period]] [[Category:Nanboku-chō period]] [[Category:Muromachi period]] [[Category:Sengoku period]] [[Category:Azuchi-Momoyama period]] [[Category:Edo period]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[sn:Samurai retainers]] [[ja:武家奉公人]] [[Category:Azuchi–Momoyama period]] [[Category:Domestic workers]] [[Category:Warrior Clans of Japan]]

They were also known as ''kobito'' or ''genan'' (下男). In the {{ill|Edo period|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, they assisted magistrates in arrest operations and were occasionally authorized to carry weapons like [[:en:Jitte|jutte]].
During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, ''komono'' served as battlefield assistants responsible for tasks like carrying supplies. == Changes in the Bakumatsu Period == In the [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] period, both the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and feudal domains abolished traditional musketeer units and established modern [[:en:Infantry|infantry]] or firearm units. Some of these units were composed of ''chūgen'' recruits, reflecting a return to the recruitment practices of the Sengoku period. == Notable Figures == * [[:en:Ninomiya Sontoku|Ninomiya Sontoku]] * {{ill|Sasaki Kengaku|ja|佐々木顕発|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasaki Kengaku|qq=|1=Sasaki Akinobu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6526523}} * [[:en:Itō Hirobumi|Itō Hirobumi]] * [[:en:Yamagata Aritomo|Yamagata Aritomo]] == See Also == * [[:en:Separation Edict|Class System Edict]] (issued by Toyotomi Hideyoshi) * [[:en:Four occupations|Shinōkōshō]] * {{ill|Hachiōji Sen'nin Dōshin|ja|八王子千人同心|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Sen'nin Dōshin|qq=|1=Hachiōji Sennin Dōshin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11391560}} * [[:en:Kabukimono|Kabukimono]] * {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate Army|ja|幕府陸軍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Shogunate Army|qq=|1=Tokugawa shogunate army|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11481798}} == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/une_gen/torimonochou.htm |title=Terminology in Crime Stories |date=20190101000000}} {{translated page|ja|武家奉公人|version=103112657|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Heian period]] [[Category:Kamakura period]] [[Category:Nanboku-chō period]] [[Category:Muromachi period]] [[Category:Sengoku period]] [[Category:Azuchi-Momoyama period]] [[Category:Edo period]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[sn:Samurai retainers]] [[ja:武家奉公人]] [[Category:Azuchi–Momoyama period]] [[Category:Domestic workers]] [[Category:Warrior Clans of Japan]] {{wikidata link|Q11545898}}

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11545898]] Wikidata name: buke hōkōnin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Citation needed|date=June 28, 2015, 13:33 (UTC)}} [[Image:Ashigalu.jpg|thumb|300px|{{ill|Ishikawa Ryusen|ja|石川流宣|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishikawa Ryusen|qq=|1=Ishikawa Tomonobu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11585888}}'s illustration of a servant in ''{{ill|Yamato Kōsaku Eshō|ja|大和耕作絵抄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kōsaku Eshō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''.]] [[File:Jidai Matsuri 2009 143.jpg|300px|thumbnail|Servants during the [[:en:Jidai Matsuri|Jidai Matsuri]]]] '''Buke Hōkōnin''' (武家奉公人) refers to retainers of peasant or townsman origin who served the {{ill|buke|ja|武家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/buke|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (samurai class). During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], such retainers were commonly referred to as simply {{ill|hōkōnin|ja|奉公人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hōkōnin|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 奉公|1=hōkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11445490}}, including samurai retainers who served their masters. These individuals primarily handled subordinate tasks for warriors, such as carrying equipment, taking care of horses, and preparing meals. In some regions, they also participated in military service. By the Edo period, the term exclusively referred to non-samurai retainers like ''chūgen'' (中間) and ''komono'' (小者). == Types == ''Buke Hōkōnin'' included Ranks such as ''wakatō,'' ''chūgen,'' and ''komono.'' However, the terms varied significantly depending on the region, domain, and household, and distinctions were not rigid. For example, ''buke hōkōnin'' could sometimes include [[:en:Ashigaru|ashigaru]], with their status either being below or above ''wakatō'' depending on the explanation.{{cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%8B%A5%E5%85%9A-153895|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240713000056/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%8B%A5%E5%85%9A-153895|title=Wakatō|publisher=Kotobank|archive-date=2024-07-13|access-date=2024-07-24}} Thus, the descriptions of ''wakatō,'' ''chūgen,'' and ''komono'' below are provided as general references. === Wakatō (若党) === The term ''wakatō'' appears in the text of [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]’s 1591 temporary [[:en:Separation Edict|class system edict]], where "samurai" (侍) is thought to refer to ''wakatō.''{{Cite journal|author=Asuka Kato|url=https://doi.org/10.24707/aichikenshikenkyu.5.0_123|title=On Toyotomi Hideyoshi's Edict of 1591|journal=Aichi Historical Studies|volume=5|pages=123–130|doi=10.24707/aichikenshikenkyu.5.0_123}}{{cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%BA%AB%E5%88%86%E7%B5%B1%E5%88%B6%E4%BB%A4-872534|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231203072152/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%BA%AB%E5%88%86%E7%B5%B1%E5%88%B6%E4%BB%A4-872534|title=Class System Edict|publisher=Kotobank|archive-date=2023-12-03|access-date=2024-07-24}} The term originally referred to young retainers, as indicated in ''{{ill|Teijō Zatsuki|ja|貞丈雑記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Teijō Zatsuki|qq=|1=Teijō Zatsuki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30933533}},'' which states: "Wakatō refers to young samurai." Over time, however, it came to denote lower-Ranking samurai retainers.''World Encyclopedia,'' Hitachi Digital Heibonsha, 1998. In the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi-Momoyama period]] and the Edo period, ''wakatō'' served as low-Ranking attendants who carried out errands and guarded their masters. They were also called ''wakatō samurai'' and wore {{ill|daishō|ja|大小|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/daishō|qq=|1=Large and Small|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10935019}} swords along with [[:en:Haori|haori]] and [[:en:Hakama|hakama]] while accompanying their masters. Their annual stipend was about three ryō and one koku, leading them to be colloquially called "three-one samurai."''Definitive Edo Period Reference,'' Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha, 1994, ASIN B00N4T23DQ. === Chūgen (中間) === ''Chūgen'' were servants of non-samurai status, distinct from ''wakatō.'' They carried only one sword, often made of wood, and were prohibited from wearing silk garments. ''Chūgen'' were typically hired on a seasonal or one-time basis, often recruited through brokers in cities like [[:en:Edo|Edo]].
Some ''chūgen'' held special roles, such as hereditary retainers in certain domains, who were permitted to bear surnames and swords.''Legacy Collection,'' Yonezawa City Library. === Komono (小者) === ''Komono'' were private servants who primarily performed miscellaneous tasks. They were also known as ''kobito'' or ''genan'' (下男). In the {{ill|Edo period|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, they assisted magistrates in arrest operations and were occasionally authorized to carry weapons like [[:en:Jitte|jutte]].
During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, ''komono'' served as battlefield assistants responsible for tasks like carrying supplies. == Changes in the Bakumatsu Period == In the [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] period, both the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and feudal domains abolished traditional musketeer units and established modern [[:en:Infantry|infantry]] or firearm units. Some of these units were composed of ''chūgen'' recruits, reflecting a return to the recruitment practices of the Sengoku period. == Notable Figures == * [[:en:Ninomiya Sontoku|Ninomiya Sontoku]] * {{ill|Sasaki Kengaku|ja|佐々木顕発|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasaki Kengaku|qq=|1=Sasaki Akinobu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6526523}} * [[:en:Itō Hirobumi|Itō Hirobumi]] * [[:en:Yamagata Aritomo|Yamagata Aritomo]] == See Also == * [[:en:Separation Edict|Class System Edict]] (issued by Toyotomi Hideyoshi) * [[:en:Four occupations|Shinōkōshō]] * {{ill|Hachiōji Sen'nin Dōshin|ja|八王子千人同心|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Sen'nin Dōshin|qq=|1=Hachiōji Sennin Dōshin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11391560}} * [[:en:Kabukimono|Kabukimono]] * {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate Army|ja|幕府陸軍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Shogunate Army|qq=|1=Tokugawa shogunate army|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11481798}} == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/une_gen/torimonochou.htm |title=Terminology in Crime Stories |date=20190101000000}} {{translated page|ja|武家奉公人|version=103112657|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Heian period]] [[Category:Kamakura period]] [[Category:Nanboku-chō period]] [[Category:Muromachi period]] [[Category:Sengoku period]] [[Category:Azuchi-Momoyama period]] [[Category:Edo period]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[ja:武家奉公人]] [[Category:Azuchi–Momoyama period]] [[Category:Domestic workers]] [[Category:Warrior Clans of Japan]] {{wikidata link|Q11545898}}
