For example, ''buke hōkōnin'' could sometimes include {{ill|Ashigaru|en|Ashigaru|WD=Q725340|lt=ashigaru}}, with their status either being below or above ''wakatō'' depending on the explanation.{{cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%8B%A5%E5%85%9A-153895|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240713000056/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%8B%A5%E5%85%9A-153895|title=Wakatō|publisher=Kotobank|archive-date=2024-07-13|access-date=2024-07-24}} Thus, the descriptions of ''wakatō,'' ''chūgen,'' and ''komono'' below are provided as general references. === Wakatō (若党) === The term ''wakatō'' appears in the text of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}’s 1591 temporary {{ill|Separation Edict|en|Separation Edict|WD=Q982467|lt=class system edict}}, where "samurai" (侍) is thought to refer to ''wakatō.''{{Cite journal|author=Asuka Kato|url=https://doi.org/10.24707/aichikenshikenkyu.5.0_123|title=On Toyotomi Hideyoshi's Edict of 1591|journal=Aichi Historical Studies|volume=5|pages=123–130|doi=10.24707/aichikenshikenkyu.5.0_123}}{{cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%BA%AB%E5%88%86%E7%B5%B1%E5%88%B6%E4%BB%A4-872534|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231203072152/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%BA%AB%E5%88%86%E7%B5%B1%E5%88%B6%E4%BB%A4-872534|title=Class System Edict|publisher=Kotobank|archive-date=2023-12-03|access-date=2024-07-24}} The term originally referred to young retainers, as indicated in ''{{ill|Teijō Zatsuki|ja|貞丈雑記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Teijō Zatsuki|1=Teijō Zatsuki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30933533}},'' which states: "Wakatō refers to young samurai." Over time, however, it came to denote lower-Ranking samurai retainers.''World Encyclopedia,'' Hitachi Digital Heibonsha, 1998. In the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|WD=Q319531|lt=Azuchi-Momoyama period}} and the Edo period, ''wakatō'' served as low-Ranking attendants who carried out errands and guarded their masters. They were also called ''wakatō samurai'' and wore {{ill|daishō|ja|大小|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/daishō|1=Large and Small|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10935019}} swords along with {{ill|Haori|en|Haori|WD=Q1503993|lt=haori}} and {{ill|Hakama|en|Hakama|WD=Q164668|lt=hakama}} while accompanying their masters. Their annual stipend was about three ryō and one koku, leading them to be colloquially called "three-one samurai."''Definitive Edo Period Reference,'' Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha, 1994, ASIN B00N4T23DQ. === Chūgen (中間) === ''Chūgen'' were servants of non-samurai status, distinct from ''wakatō.'' They carried only one sword, often made of wood, and were prohibited from wearing silk garments. ''Chūgen'' were typically hired on a seasonal or one-time basis, often recruited through brokers in cities like {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo}}.
Some ''chūgen'' held special roles, such as hereditary retainers in certain domains, who were permitted to bear surnames and swords.''Legacy Collection,'' Yonezawa City Library. === Komono (小者) === ''Komono'' were private servants who primarily performed miscellaneous tasks. They were also known as ''kobito'' or ''genan'' (下男). In the {{ill|Edo period|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, they assisted magistrates in arrest operations and were occasionally authorized to carry weapons like {{ill|Jitte|en|Jitte|WD=Q773214|lt=jutte}}.
During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, ''komono'' served as battlefield assistants responsible for tasks like carrying supplies. == Changes in the Bakumatsu Period == In the {{ill|Bakumatsu|en|Bakumatsu|WD=Q804558|lt=Bakumatsu}} period, both the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and feudal domains abolished traditional musketeer units and established modern {{ill|Infantry|en|Infantry|WD=Q29171|lt=infantry}} or firearm units. Some of these units were composed of ''chūgen'' recruits, reflecting a return to the recruitment practices of the Sengoku period. == Notable Figures == * {{ill|Ninomiya Sontoku|en|Ninomiya Sontoku|WD=Q968964|lt=Ninomiya Sontoku}} * {{ill|Sasaki Kengaku|ja|佐々木顕発|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasaki Kengaku|1=Sasaki Akinobu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6526523}} * {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|en|Itō Hirobumi|WD=Q174971|lt=Itō Hirobumi}} * {{ill|Yamagata Aritomo|en|Yamagata Aritomo|WD=Q297636|lt=Yamagata Aritomo}} == See Also == * {{ill|Separation Edict|en|Separation Edict|WD=Q982467|lt=Class System Edict}} (issued by Toyotomi Hideyoshi) * {{ill|Four occupations|en|Four occupations|WD=Q1328489|lt=Shinōkōshō}} * {{ill|Hachiōji Sen'nin Dōshin|ja|八王子千人同心|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Sen'nin Dōshin|1=Hachiōji Sennin Dōshin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11391560}} * {{ill|Kabukimono|en|Kabukimono|WD=Q592897|lt=Kabukimono}} * {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate Army|ja|幕府陸軍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Shogunate Army|1=Tokugawa shogunate army|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11481798}} == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/une_gen/torimonochou.htm |title=Terminology in Crime Stories |date=20190101000000}} {{translated page|ja|武家奉公人|version=103112657|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:武家奉公人]] {{wikidata link|Q11545898}} [[Category:Azuchi-Momoyama period]] [[Category:Azuchi–Momoyama period]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Domestic workers]] [[Category:Edo period]] [[Category:Heian period]] [[Category:Kamakura period]] [[Category:Muromachi period]] [[Category:Nanboku-chō period]] [[Category:Sengoku period]] [[Category:Warrior Clans of Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

[[ja:武家奉公人]] [[ja:武家奉公人]] [[ja:武家奉公人]] {{Citation needed|date=June 28, 2015, 13:33 (UTC)}} [[Image:Ashigalu.jpg|thumb|300px|{{ill|Ishikawa Ryusen|ja|石川流宣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishikawa Ryusen|1=Ishikawa Tomonobu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11585888}}'s illustration of a servant in ''{{ill|Yamato Kōsaku Eshō|ja|大和耕作絵抄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kōsaku Eshō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''.]] [[File:Jidai Matsuri 2009 143.jpg|300px|thumbnail|Servants during the {{ill|Jidai Matsuri|en|Jidai Matsuri|WD=Q912124|lt=Jidai Matsuri}}]] '''Buke Hōkōnin''' (武家奉公人) refers to retainers of peasant or townsman origin who served the {{ill|buke|ja|武家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/buke|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (samurai class). During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, such retainers were commonly referred to as simply {{ill|hōkōnin|ja|奉公人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hōkōnin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 奉公|1=hōkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11445490}}, including samurai retainers who served their masters. These individuals primarily handled subordinate tasks for warriors, such as carrying equipment, taking care of horses, and preparing meals. In some regions, they also participated in military service. By the Edo period, the term exclusively referred to non-samurai retainers like ''chūgen'' (中間) and ''komono'' (小者). == Types == ''Buke Hōkōnin'' included Ranks such as ''wakatō,'' ''chūgen,'' and ''komono.'' However, the terms varied significantly depending on the region, domain, and household, and distinctions were not rigid. For example, ''buke hōkōnin'' could sometimes include {{ill|Ashigaru|en|Ashigaru|WD=Q725340|lt=ashigaru}}, with their status either being below or above ''wakatō'' depending on the explanation.{{cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%8B%A5%E5%85%9A-153895|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240713000056/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%8B%A5%E5%85%9A-153895|title=Wakatō|publisher=Kotobank|archive-date=2024-07-13|access-date=2024-07-24}} Thus, the descriptions of ''wakatō,'' ''chūgen,'' and ''komono'' below are provided as general references. === Wakatō (若党) === The term ''wakatō'' appears in the text of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}’s 1591 temporary {{ill|Separation Edict|en|Separation Edict|WD=Q982467|lt=class system edict}}, where "samurai" (侍) is thought to refer to ''wakatō.''{{Cite journal|author=Asuka Kato|url=https://doi.org/10.24707/aichikenshikenkyu.5.0_123|title=On Toyotomi Hideyoshi's Edict of 1591|journal=Aichi Historical Studies|volume=5|pages=123–130|doi=10.24707/aichikenshikenkyu.5.0_123}}{{cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%BA%AB%E5%88%86%E7%B5%B1%E5%88%B6%E4%BB%A4-872534|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231203072152/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%BA%AB%E5%88%86%E7%B5%B1%E5%88%B6%E4%BB%A4-872534|title=Class System Edict|publisher=Kotobank|archive-date=2023-12-03|access-date=2024-07-24}} The term originally referred to young retainers, as indicated in ''{{ill|Teijō Zatsuki|ja|貞丈雑記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Teijō Zatsuki|1=Teijō Zatsuki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30933533}},'' which states: "Wakatō refers to young samurai." Over time, however, it came to denote lower-Ranking samurai retainers.''World Encyclopedia,'' Hitachi Digital Heibonsha, 1998. In the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|WD=Q319531|lt=Azuchi-Momoyama period}} and the Edo period, ''wakatō'' served as low-Ranking attendants who carried out errands and guarded their masters. They were also called ''wakatō samurai'' and wore {{ill|daishō|ja|大小|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/daishō|1=Large and Small|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10935019}} swords along with {{ill|Haori|en|Haori|WD=Q1503993|lt=haori}} and {{ill|Hakama|en|Hakama|WD=Q164668|lt=hakama}} while accompanying their masters. Their annual stipend was about three ryō and one koku, leading them to be colloquially called "three-one samurai."''Definitive Edo Period Reference,'' Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha, 1994, ASIN B00N4T23DQ. === Chūgen (中間) === ''Chūgen'' were servants of non-samurai status, distinct from ''wakatō.'' They carried only one sword, often made of wood, and were prohibited from wearing silk garments. ''Chūgen'' were typically hired on a seasonal or one-time basis, often recruited through brokers in cities like {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo}}.
Some ''chūgen'' held special roles, such as hereditary retainers in certain domains, who were permitted to bear surnames and swords.''Legacy Collection,'' Yonezawa City Library. === Komono (小者) === ''Komono'' were private servants who primarily performed miscellaneous tasks. They were also known as ''kobito'' or ''genan'' (下男). In the {{ill|Edo period|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, they assisted magistrates in arrest operations and were occasionally authorized to carry weapons like {{ill|Jitte|en|Jitte|WD=Q773214|lt=jutte}}.
During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, ''komono'' served as battlefield assistants responsible for tasks like carrying supplies. == Changes in the Bakumatsu Period == In the {{ill|Bakumatsu|en|Bakumatsu|WD=Q804558|lt=Bakumatsu}} period, both the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and feudal domains abolished traditional musketeer units and established modern {{ill|Infantry|en|Infantry|WD=Q29171|lt=infantry}} or firearm units. Some of these units were composed of ''chūgen'' recruits, reflecting a return to the recruitment practices of the Sengoku period. == Notable Figures == * {{ill|Ninomiya Sontoku|en|Ninomiya Sontoku|WD=Q968964|lt=Ninomiya Sontoku}} * {{ill|Sasaki Kengaku|ja|佐々木顕発|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasaki Kengaku|1=Sasaki Akinobu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6526523}} * {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|en|Itō Hirobumi|WD=Q174971|lt=Itō Hirobumi}} * {{ill|Yamagata Aritomo|en|Yamagata Aritomo|WD=Q297636|lt=Yamagata Aritomo}} == See Also == * {{ill|Separation Edict|en|Separation Edict|WD=Q982467|lt=Class System Edict}} (issued by Toyotomi Hideyoshi) * {{ill|Four occupations|en|Four occupations|WD=Q1328489|lt=Shinōkōshō}} * {{ill|Hachiōji Sen'nin Dōshin|ja|八王子千人同心|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Sen'nin Dōshin|1=Hachiōji Sennin Dōshin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11391560}} * {{ill|Kabukimono|en|Kabukimono|WD=Q592897|lt=Kabukimono}} * {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate Army|ja|幕府陸軍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Shogunate Army|1=Tokugawa shogunate army|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11481798}} == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/une_gen/torimonochou.htm |title=Terminology in Crime Stories |date=20190101000000}} {{translated page|ja|武家奉公人|version=103112657|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:武家奉公人]] {{wikidata link|Q11545898}} [[Category:Azuchi-Momoyama period]] [[Category:Azuchi–Momoyama period]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Domestic workers]] [[Category:Edo period]] [[Category:Heian period]] [[Category:Kamakura period]] [[Category:Muromachi period]] [[Category:Nanboku-chō period]] [[Category:Sengoku period]] [[Category:Warrior Clans of Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages]]

During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, ''komono'' served as battlefield assistants responsible for tasks like carrying supplies. == Changes in the Bakumatsu Period == In the {{ill|Bakumatsu|en|Bakumatsu|WD=Q804558|lt=Bakumatsu|1=Bakumatsu}} period, both the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and feudal domains abolished traditional musketeer units and established modern {{ill|Infantry|en|Infantry|WD=Q29171|lt=infantry|1=Infantry}} or firearm units. Some of these units were composed of ''chūgen'' recruits, reflecting a return to the recruitment practices of the Sengoku period. == Notable Figures == * {{ill|Ninomiya Sontoku|en|Ninomiya Sontoku|WD=Q968964|lt=Ninomiya Sontoku|1=Ninomiya Sontoku}} * {{ill|Sasaki Kengaku|ja|佐々木顕発|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasaki Kengaku|1=Sasaki Akinobu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6526523}} * {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|en|Itō Hirobumi|WD=Q174971|lt=Itō Hirobumi|1=Itō Hirobumi}} * {{ill|Yamagata Aritomo|en|Yamagata Aritomo|WD=Q297636|lt=Yamagata Aritomo|1=Yamagata Aritomo}} == See Also == * {{ill|Separation Edict|en|Separation Edict|WD=Q982467|lt=Class System Edict|1=Separation Edict}} (issued by Toyotomi Hideyoshi) * {{ill|Four occupations|en|Four occupations|WD=Q1328489|lt=Shinōkōshō|1=Four occupations}} * {{ill|Hachiōji Sen'nin Dōshin|ja|八王子千人同心|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Sen'nin Dōshin|1=Hachiōji Sennin Dōshin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11391560}} * {{ill|Kabukimono|en|Kabukimono|WD=Q592897|lt=Kabukimono|1=Kabukimono}} * {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate Army|ja|幕府陸軍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Shogunate Army|1=Tokugawa shogunate army|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11481798}} == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/une_gen/torimonochou.htm |title=Terminology in Crime Stories |date=20190101000000}} {{translated page|ja|武家奉公人|version=103112657|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:武家奉公人]] {{wikidata link|Q11545898}} [[Category:Azuchi-Momoyama period]] [[Category:Azuchi–Momoyama period]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Domestic workers]] [[Category:Edo period]] [[Category:Heian period]] [[Category:Kamakura period]] [[Category:Muromachi period]] [[Category:Nanboku-chō period]] [[Category:Sengoku period]] [[Category:Warrior Clans of Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages]]

[[ja:武家奉公人]] [[ja:武家奉公人]] [[ja:武家奉公人]] {{Citation needed|date=June 28, 2015, 13:33 (UTC)}} [[Image:Ashigalu.jpg|thumb|300px|{{ill|Ishikawa Ryusen|ja|石川流宣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishikawa Ryusen|1=Ishikawa Tomonobu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11585888}}'s illustration of a servant in ''{{ill|Yamato Kōsaku Eshō|ja|大和耕作絵抄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kōsaku Eshō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''.]] [[File:Jidai Matsuri 2009 143.jpg|300px|thumbnail|Servants during the {{ill|Jidai Matsuri|en|Jidai Matsuri|WD=Q912124|lt=Jidai Matsuri|1=Jidai Matsuri}}]] '''Buke Hōkōnin''' (武家奉公人) refers to retainers of peasant or townsman origin who served the {{ill|buke|ja|武家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/buke|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (samurai class). During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, such retainers were commonly referred to as simply {{ill|hōkōnin|ja|奉公人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hōkōnin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 奉公|1=hōkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11445490}}, including samurai retainers who served their masters. These individuals primarily handled subordinate tasks for warriors, such as carrying equipment, taking care of horses, and preparing meals. In some regions, they also participated in military service. By the Edo period, the term exclusively referred to non-samurai retainers like ''chūgen'' (中間) and ''komono'' (小者). == Types == ''Buke Hōkōnin'' included Ranks such as ''wakatō,'' ''chūgen,'' and ''komono.'' However, the terms varied significantly depending on the region, domain, and household, and distinctions were not rigid. For example, ''buke hōkōnin'' could sometimes include {{ill|Ashigaru|en|Ashigaru|WD=Q725340|lt=ashigaru|1=Ashigaru}}, with their status either being below or above ''wakatō'' depending on the explanation.{{cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%8B%A5%E5%85%9A-153895|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240713000056/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%8B%A5%E5%85%9A-153895|title=Wakatō|publisher=Kotobank|archive-date=2024-07-13|access-date=2024-07-24}} Thus, the descriptions of ''wakatō,'' ''chūgen,'' and ''komono'' below are provided as general references. === Wakatō (若党) === The term ''wakatō'' appears in the text of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}’s 1591 temporary {{ill|Separation Edict|en|Separation Edict|WD=Q982467|lt=class system edict|1=Separation Edict}}, where "samurai" (侍) is thought to refer to ''wakatō.''{{Cite journal|author=Asuka Kato|url=https://doi.org/10.24707/aichikenshikenkyu.5.0_123|title=On Toyotomi Hideyoshi's Edict of 1591|journal=Aichi Historical Studies|volume=5|pages=123–130|doi=10.24707/aichikenshikenkyu.5.0_123}}{{cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%BA%AB%E5%88%86%E7%B5%B1%E5%88%B6%E4%BB%A4-872534|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231203072152/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%BA%AB%E5%88%86%E7%B5%B1%E5%88%B6%E4%BB%A4-872534|title=Class System Edict|publisher=Kotobank|archive-date=2023-12-03|access-date=2024-07-24}} The term originally referred to young retainers, as indicated in ''{{ill|Teijō Zatsuki|ja|貞丈雑記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Teijō Zatsuki|1=Q30933533|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30933533}},'' which states: "Wakatō refers to young samurai." Over time, however, it came to denote lower-Ranking samurai retainers.''World Encyclopedia,'' Hitachi Digital Heibonsha, 1998. In the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|WD=Q319531|lt=Azuchi-Momoyama period|1=Azuchi–Momoyama period}} and the Edo period, ''wakatō'' served as low-Ranking attendants who carried out errands and guarded their masters. They were also called ''wakatō samurai'' and wore {{ill|daishō|ja|大小|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/daishō|1=Large and Small|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10935019}} swords along with {{ill|Haori|en|Haori|WD=Q1503993|lt=haori|1=Haori}} and {{ill|Hakama|en|Hakama|WD=Q164668|lt=hakama|1=Hakama}} while accompanying their masters. Their annual stipend was about three ryō and one koku, leading them to be colloquially called "three-one samurai."''Definitive Edo Period Reference,'' Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha, 1994, ASIN B00N4T23DQ. === Chūgen (中間) === ''Chūgen'' were servants of non-samurai status, distinct from ''wakatō.'' They carried only one sword, often made of wood, and were prohibited from wearing silk garments. ''Chūgen'' were typically hired on a seasonal or one-time basis, often recruited through brokers in cities like {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo|1=Edo}}.
Some ''chūgen'' held special roles, such as hereditary retainers in certain domains, who were permitted to bear surnames and swords.''Legacy Collection,'' Yonezawa City Library. === Komono (小者) === ''Komono'' were private servants who primarily performed miscellaneous tasks. They were also known as ''kobito'' or ''genan'' (下男). In the {{ill|Edo period|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, they assisted magistrates in arrest operations and were occasionally authorized to carry weapons like {{ill|Jitte|en|Jitte|WD=Q773214|lt=jutte|1=Jitte}}.
During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, ''komono'' served as battlefield assistants responsible for tasks like carrying supplies. == Changes in the Bakumatsu Period == In the {{ill|Bakumatsu|en|Bakumatsu|WD=Q804558|lt=Bakumatsu|1=Bakumatsu}} period, both the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and feudal domains abolished traditional musketeer units and established modern {{ill|Infantry|en|Infantry|WD=Q29171|lt=infantry|1=Infantry}} or firearm units. Some of these units were composed of ''chūgen'' recruits, reflecting a return to the recruitment practices of the Sengoku period. == Notable Figures == * {{ill|Ninomiya Sontoku|en|Ninomiya Sontoku|WD=Q968964|lt=Ninomiya Sontoku|1=Ninomiya Sontoku}} * {{ill|Sasaki Kengaku|ja|佐々木顕発|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasaki Kengaku|1=Sasaki Akinobu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6526523}} * {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|en|Itō Hirobumi|WD=Q174971|lt=Itō Hirobumi|1=Itō Hirobumi}} * {{ill|Yamagata Aritomo|en|Yamagata Aritomo|WD=Q297636|lt=Yamagata Aritomo|1=Yamagata Aritomo}} == See Also == * {{ill|Separation Edict|en|Separation Edict|WD=Q982467|lt=Class System Edict|1=Separation Edict}} (issued by Toyotomi Hideyoshi) * {{ill|Four occupations|en|Four occupations|WD=Q1328489|lt=Shinōkōshō|1=Four occupations}} * {{ill|Hachiōji Sen'nin Dōshin|ja|八王子千人同心|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Sen'nin Dōshin|1=Hachiōji Sennin Dōshin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11391560}} * {{ill|Kabukimono|en|Kabukimono|WD=Q592897|lt=Kabukimono|1=Kabukimono}} * {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate Army|ja|幕府陸軍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Shogunate Army|1=Tokugawa shogunate army|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11481798}} == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/une_gen/torimonochou.htm |title=Terminology in Crime Stories |date=20190101000000}} {{translated page|ja|武家奉公人|version=103112657|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:武家奉公人]] {{wikidata link|Q11545898}} [[Category:Azuchi-Momoyama period]] [[Category:Azuchi–Momoyama period]] [[Category:Domestic workers]] [[Category:Edo period]] [[Category:Heian period]] [[Category:Kamakura period]] [[Category:Muromachi period]] [[Category:Nanboku-chō period]] [[Category:Sengoku period]] [[Category:Warrior Clans of Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages]]
